rs2228570.name: Vitamin D Receptor
rs2228570.display: VDR — Vitamin D Receptor (FokI, Transcriptional Activity)

GG.effect: F/F — longer, less efficient VDR protein
GG.interpretation: For vitamin d receptor, your VDR variant is one contributing factor — foki 'f' allele produces longer vdr that initiates at met1 — slightly less transcriptionally efficient; may require higher vitamin d levels for equivalent signaling This is a statistical association that interacts with lifestyle, environment, and other genetic factors.
recommendations: Vitamin D3 3000-4000IU and target 50-70 ng/mL 25-OH-D
Magnesium 400mg co-supplementation for VDR activation

AG.interpretation: For vitamin d receptor, your VDR variant is one contributing factor — heterozygous foki — intermediate vitamin d receptor activity.
recommendations: Standard vitamin D optimization protocol (target 40-60 ng/mL)
Track 25(OH)D levels and adjust supplementation accordingly

AA.effect: f/f — shorter VDR protein, higher transcriptional activity
AA.interpretation: FokI 'f' allele produces shorter VDR protein initiating at Met4 instead of Met1 — functions more efficiently in transcription; linked to better immune response, vitamin D efficiency, and possibly lower cancer risk
recommendations: Lower supplemental vitamin D3 may be needed compared to FF genotype (higher transcriptional efficiency)
Target 40-60 ng/mL 25-OH-D rather than aggressive 70 ng/mL target

GA.interpretation: For vitamin d receptor, your VDR variant is one contributing factor — one shorter vdr variant — intermediate transcriptional activity
recommendations: Standard vitamin D3 2000-3000IU supplementation

recommendations: Vitamin D3 2000-4000IU maintain 50-70 ng/mL serum 25-OH-D
Vitamin K2 MK-7 100mcg co-supplementation
Magnesium 400mg (VDR activation requires magnesium)

TA.interpretation: For apai, your VDR variant is one contributing factor — heterozygous vdr apai — intermediate haplotype effect on receptor expression
recommendations: Standard vitamin D optimization

AA.interpretation: For apai, your VDR variant is one contributing factor — wild-type apai allele — standard vdr transcript regulation
recommendations: Standard vitamin D intake and sunlight exposure

rs2104772.name: Tendinopathies in lower extremities (legs)

T/T.interpretation: Population studies link your TNC variant to altered probability for tendinopathies in lower extremities (legs). This reflects group-level statistics; individual risk depends substantially on lifestyle, family history, and other genes.
recommendations: Discuss tendinopathies in lower extremities (legs) screening with a healthcare provider if family history is present
Lifestyle modifications often reduce genetic risk — diet, exercise, and avoiding known triggers matter

rs2104772.technical_report:
Several studies have identified 7 markers associated with the risk of tendinopathies and
ligament ruptures in the lower extremities. These markers are mainly found in genes such
as COL1A1, COL5A1, GDF5, TIMP2, TNC, ADAMTS14 and MMP3. These genes are involved
in the maintenance, development and repair of bone, cartilage and other soft
musculoskeletal tissues, such as tendons and ligaments. Type I collagen is the major protein that makes up tendons and ligaments. It is composed of
chains or fibrils encoded by the COL1A1 and COL1A2 genes. It is known that mutations in
the COL1A1 gene can cause connective tissue diseases such as osteogenesis imperfecta or
Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, as well as other less dramatic tendon and ligament disorders that
can be related to athletic performance. The ADAMTS14 gene produces an enzyme involved
in the process of collagen synthesis. GDF5 encodes for a growth factor that is essential for bone and cartilage development. The MMP3 protein mediates degrading components of the extracellular matrix of
tissues and is part of the metalloproteinase family. Therefore, variants in the MMP3 gene
can affect the breakdown of soft tissues such as tendons and ligaments. The TIMP2 gene
produces a small protein that binds to metalloproteinases and inhibits their functions. The TNC (tenascin-C) gene encodes a protein involved in cell adhesion to the matrix. Genetic variants in TNC can affect the elasticity conferred by tenascin-C to tissues, thereby
affecting wound healing and tissue remodeling.
rs2104772.description:
PDF

Tendinopathies in lower extremities (legs)
The probability of suffering tendinopathy in the lower extremities, in addition to being
affected by a multitude of environmental factors such as exercise, is also affected by the
genetic component. Variants in the genes that regulate the development and maintenance
of bones, cartilage and tendons play a fundamental role in the vulnerability of certain
individuals to injury.

rs12722.name: Tendinopathies in lower extremities (legs)

C/T.interpretation: Tendinopathies in lower extremities (legs) — your COL5A1 genotype suggests a favorable profile that may confer some natural advantage. This is a statistical association from population studies, not a guarantee of any outcome.
recommendations: Continue healthy lifestyle habits that support tendinopathies in lower extremities (legs)
Genotype alone does not override environmental and behavioral factors

TT.effect: Two T alleles at COL5A1 rs12722
TT.interpretation: For tendinopathies in lower extremities (legs), your COL5A1 variant is one contributing factor — some meta-analyses associate the tt genotype with tendon or ligament injury in selected cohorts, while results differ by ancestry, injury site, and study design
recommendations: Base load progression on training history, pain, biomechanics, and recovery
Use a clinician or physiotherapist for persistent tendon or ligament symptoms

CT.effect: One C allele and one T allele
CT.interpretation: For tendinopathies in lower extremities (legs), your COL5A1 variant is one contributing factor — association studies do not support an individual intermediate-stiffness label
recommendations: Use progressive loading and track symptoms regardless of genotype

CC.effect: Two C alleles at COL5A1 rs12722
CC.interpretation: Genotype CC may contribute to not a diagnosis of compliant tendons or high injury risk
recommendations: Use the same evidence-based load management and symptom Tracking as any athlete

rs1800972.name: Tendinopathies in upper extremities (arms)

G/G.interpretation: Your DEFB1 genotype is the most common form in reference populations. This does not rule out tendinopathies in upper extremities (arms) risk from other genes, environmental exposures, or rare variants not covered by standard genotyping.
recommendations: No specific DEFB1-directed intervention indicated, but standard health screening still applies
Other genetic and environmental factors contribute to tendinopathies in upper extremities (arms) — genotype alone is not the full picture

bibliography: Khoschnau S, Melhus H, Jacobson A, Rahme H, Bengtsson H, Ribom E, et al. Type I Collagen ?1 Sp1
Polymorphism and the Risk of Cruciate Ligament Ruptures or Shoulder Dislocations. Am J Sports Med,

in tenascin-C (TNC) associated with degenerative rotator cuff tears. J Orthop Res, 2017; 35(4):894901.
Motta G da R, Amaral MV, Rezende E, Pitta R, dos Santos Vieira TC, Duarte MEL, et al. Evidence of genetic

Vaughn NH, Stepanyan H, Gallo RA, Dhawan A. Genetic Factors in Tendon Injury: A Systematic Review of
the Literature. Orthop J Sport Med, 2017; 5(8):232596711772441.
rs1800972.technical_report:
Several studies have identified 3 markers associated with the risk of tendon injuries in
rotator cuffs. These markers are found in COL1A1, a gene that codes for type I collagen and
whose variants have been associated with tendinopathies and ligament ruptures, as well as
other connective tissue pathologies (osteogenesis imperfecta and Ehlers-Danlos
syndrome). We can also find the DEFB1 gene, which produces the beta-defensin 1 protein,
an antimicrobial peptide expressed on epithelial surfaces and which is also implicated in
other pathologies such as muscular dystrophy and cystic fibrosis. The third gene analyzed
is the tenascin-C gene (TNC). Certain variants in TNC may contribute to a greater or lesser
risk of injury to the tendons that attach the arm to the shoulder and that occur frequently
in athletes.
rs1800972.description:
PDF

Tendinopathies in upper extremities (arms)
Tendons are anatomical structures located between the muscle and the bone, whose
function is to transmit the force generated by the muscle to the bone, giving rise to joint
movement. The influence of genetics in tendon pathologies is increasingly known, which
allows a more exhaustive knowledge of the interpretation of genetic variations, not only in
the causes of tendinopathies but also in other aspects such as susceptibility, prognosis and
individual response to treatments.

Other genetic and environmental factors contribute to tendinopathies in upper extremities (arms) — genotype alone is not the full picture
